summary: Julia Evans shares her approach to tech blogging, explaining her motivations evolved from job-hunting strategy to learning tool and community builder. She advocates for writing short posts quickly (1-3 hours), publishing frequently without excessive editing, and maintaining a low bar for what counts as 'interesting enough' to write about. She addresses the fear of admitting ignorance publicly, arguing it actually accelerates learning. Her core philosophy treats blog posts as small lottery tickets — produce many, keep them short, and let volume do the work.
key_points: Writing short posts (under 500 words) with high frequency is more effective than polishing long-form pieces, and the volume increases the odds of producing something great
Admitting what you don't know in public writing accelerates learning because people help fill your gaps, and the fear of looking ignorant is mostly unfounded
The litmus test for whether something is worth writing about is simple: would this have helped me a year ago, or last week, or be interesting to at least two other people?
Maintaining an aggressively positive tone serves both as genuine expression and as a defensive tactic against internet negativity
Blogging compounds over time — 200 posts exist permanently and continue providing value long after they're written
money_quotes: I sit down and write something and don't stop until I've published it.
Producing things that you think are good is like buying a lottery ticket. So I guess I try to make good things and buy many small lottery tickets.
Basically I have a pretty low bar for interestingness and it mostly works out fine.
Nobody ever thought I was a magical wizard who knows everything in the first place.
Telling people what I don't know means that they can help me learn it sooner!
On the balance I think I'd happy be known for being curious and knowledgeable and a good source of information & questions and that I don't need to be a magical wizard oracle.
I also think it's extremely fun and interesting when regular people (not Programming Wizards) just write about their regular work and what they're learning right now.
key_insight: Prolific, honest blogging that embraces ignorance and keeps a low bar for interestingness compounds into career capital and genuine learning far more effectively than polished, infrequent writing.
